meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Azure AI Apps and Agents Developer Associate (AI-103)
meta.title: MS-AI103-1: Foundry Agents, RAG and Responsible AI

questions.stem: A team wants to build a customer support chatbot that answers questions about their product documentation. The documentation is updated weekly. Which approach is MOST appropriate for keeping the chatbot's knowledge current without retraining a model?

options.text: Fine-tune an open-source LLM on the documentation corpus each week
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with a vector index refreshed from the updated documentation
Include the full documentation in every prompt to the LLM
Use a proprietary LLM with a large context window so no retrieval is needed

questions.explanation: B is correct because RAG decouples the knowledge base from the model weights, allowing the vector index to be refreshed with new documents without retraining. A is wrong because fine-tuning weekly is expensive and slow, and fine-tuned knowledge can still become stale. C is wrong because documentation corpora are typically too large to fit in any context window, and cost per query would be prohibitive. D is wrong because even large context windows cannot hold enterprise documentation, and the model's parametric knowledge would still be static.
questions.reference: RAG Architecture: retriever + generator pattern

questions.stem: An engineer is designing a RAG pipeline. After evaluating the system, they find that retrieved chunks are often irrelevant to the user's query. Which of the following changes to the chunking strategy is MOST likely to improve retrieval precision?

options.text: Increase chunk size from 128 tokens to 1024 tokens to capture more context
Switch from fixed-size chunking to semantic chunking that splits on natural topic boundaries
Reduce chunk overlap from 20 tokens to 0 tokens to avoid duplicate content
Increase the number of retrieved chunks (top-k) from 3 to 20

questions.explanation: B is correct because semantic chunking creates chunks that correspond to coherent topics, making each chunk more likely to match a specific type of query. A is wrong because very large chunks reduce precision — a 1024-token chunk may contain many topics and will be retrieved for queries that only match a small portion of it. C is wrong because removing overlap can cause important context that spans chunk boundaries to be missed. D is wrong because increasing top-k adds noise by pulling in more potentially irrelevant chunks, hurting precision even if recall improves.
questions.reference: Chunking strategies: fixed-size vs semantic chunking

questions.stem: A Azure AI developer is configuring a Azure AI Search vector index index. The documents are updated continuously in a knowledge-store dataset, and the engineer wants index changes to propagate automatically. Which index type should they choose?

options.text: application-managed vector index
indexer-driven vector index
Flat L2 index
HNSW approximate index

questions.explanation: B is correct because a indexer-driven vector index automatically syncs with the source knowledge-store dataset when it changes, including through triggered or continuous pipeline mode. A is wrong because a application-managed vector index requires the caller to manage upserts/deletes to the index directly via API — there is no automatic sync from a knowledge-store dataset. C and D are wrong because these describe similarity algorithm types, not Azure AI Search vector index index types.
questions.reference: Vector search design: Azure AI Search vector index index types (delta sync vs direct vector access)

questions.stem: A team is evaluating embedding similarity metrics for a legal document search system. Documents range from 1,000 to 5,000 words. Which similarity metric is generally preferred for normalized embedding vectors and why?

options.text: L2 (Euclidean) distance, because it captures absolute positional differences between vectors
Dot product, because it is computationally cheaper than cosine similarity
Cosine similarity, because it measures the angle between vectors and is invariant to vector magnitude
Jaccard similarity, because it accounts for token overlap between document embeddings

questions.explanation: C is correct because cosine similarity measures the angular distance between vectors, making it invariant to the magnitude of the embedding. For text similarity, documents of different lengths can still be semantically close even if their raw vector magnitudes differ. B is partially correct that dot product is cheaper, and for unit-normalized vectors dot product equals cosine similarity — but when vectors are not normalized, dot product conflates magnitude with direction. A is wrong because L2 distance is magnitude-sensitive and less appropriate for semantic text search. D is wrong because Jaccard similarity applies to set-based representations (e.g., bag of words), not dense embedding vectors.
questions.reference: Vector search design: similarity metrics (cosine, dot product, L2)

questions.stem: A startup wants to deploy a GenAI application with the lowest possible inference cost and does not require top-tier response quality. Which LLM selection strategy is MOST aligned with these goals?

options.text: GPT-4 via Azure AI Foundry AI Gateway for maximum quality
A small open-source model (e.g., Mistral 7B) deployed on Azure AI Foundry Model Serving with pay-per-token pricing
Claude Opus via Azure AI Foundry AI Gateway for its large context window
Fine-tune GPT-4 on proprietary data to improve quality and reduce call frequency

questions.explanation: B is correct because smaller open-source models like Mistral 7B have dramatically lower inference costs and can be hosted on smaller GPU instances. Pay-per-token external models like GPT-4 or Claude Opus (A, C) are expensive for high-volume use cases. D is wrong because GPT-4 fine-tuning is not available in the same way as open-source models and would still be expensive at inference time.
questions.reference: LLM selection: open-source vs proprietary, model size tradeoffs

questions.stem: A team is designing a RAG system for a technical knowledge base. Select TWO factors that most directly affect the quality of embedding-based retrieval. (Select TWO)

options.text: The embedding model used to encode queries and documents
The number of GPU cores on the vector search server
The chunking strategy and chunk size used during document ingestion
The temperature parameter of the generator LLM

questions.explanation: A is correct because the embedding model determines the quality of vector representations — a domain-relevant model (e.g., bge-large) will produce better representations than a generic one. C is correct because chunk size and strategy directly affect what information is in each retrievable unit — too small loses context, too large reduces precision. B is wrong because GPU cores affect latency and throughput but not retrieval quality. D is wrong because the generator's temperature controls output randomness and has no effect on the retrieval step.
questions.reference: RAG components: embedding, chunking strategies

questions.stem: A multi-turn customer support bot stores the full conversation history in the prompt. After 20 turns, users report that the bot starts ignoring earlier parts of the conversation. What is the MOST likely cause and the best remediation?

options.text: The LLM temperature is too high; reduce it to 0.1 to improve coherence
The conversation history exceeds the model's context window; implement conversation summarization to compress earlier turns
The system prompt is too long; shorten it to leave room for conversation history
The embedding model cannot represent long conversations; switch to a larger embedding model

questions.explanation: B is correct because LLMs have a fixed context window (e.g., 4K, 8K, 32K tokens), and as conversation history grows, earlier turns get truncated or the model's attention is diluted across too many tokens — causing it to 'forget' early context. The standard mitigation is to summarize older turns into a running summary. A is wrong because temperature affects creativity/randomness of responses, not memory of prior context. C is wrong because while shortening the system prompt helps, it does not address the root cause of unbounded history growth. D is wrong because embeddings are not involved in multi-turn conversation history management.
questions.reference: Multi-turn conversations: context window limits, summarization of history

questions.stem: An engineer is choosing between RAG and fine-tuning for a use case where an LLM needs to respond in a very specific brand voice and follow strict output format rules (e.g., always respond in JSON with specific field names). The underlying facts change infrequently. Which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.text: RAG only, because it is always better than fine-tuning for production systems
Fine-tuning only, because the model needs to learn style and format patterns rather than new factual knowledge
RAG only, because retrieved context can provide the format examples the model needs
Neither; use a rules engine to enforce output format instead of an LLM

questions.explanation: B is correct because fine-tuning is best suited for teaching a model behavioral patterns — style, tone, and structured output formats — that are consistent across many interactions. RAG addresses the problem of providing up-to-date factual knowledge at inference time, but it does not teach the model to reliably follow a new output schema. A is wrong because RAG is not universally superior to fine-tuning; the choice depends on the use case. C is wrong because while few-shot examples in retrieved context can help, they are inconsistent and not as reliable as fine-tuning for strict format adherence. D is wrong because a rules engine cannot handle the natural language understanding component of the task.
questions.reference: RAG vs fine-tuning: when to use each approach

questions.stem: A Azure AI Search vector index index is configured with approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. A data scientist notices that some highly relevant documents are not being returned even though they exist in the index. What is the MOST likely explanation?

options.text: The documents were chunked with overlapping tokens, causing them to be deduplicated during indexing
ANN search trades recall for speed and may not return all true nearest neighbors; switching to exact search or increasing the efSearch parameter may help
The embedding model used at query time differs from the one used at indexing time
Cosine similarity does not support documents longer than 512 tokens

questions.explanation: B is correct because approximate nearest neighbor algorithms (like HNSW) use graph-based shortcuts to achieve fast search at the cost of recall — they may miss some true nearest neighbors. Increasing the search expansion parameter (efSearch in HNSW) explores more candidates and improves recall at the cost of latency. C is also a real problem (mismatched embedding models would cause poor results), but the question states documents exist in the index — a model mismatch would affect all queries, not just some documents. A is wrong because chunk overlap does not cause deduplication. D is wrong because cosine similarity has no document length limit.
questions.reference: Vector search design: approximate vs exact search

questions.stem: A data engineer is building a RAG pipeline and needs to load text from a collection of PDF files stored in a cloud object store. Which Azure AI Foundry approach is MOST appropriate for parallelizing this ingestion at scale?

options.text: Use a single-node Python script with PyPDF2 to extract text sequentially
Use a Spark UDF that applies a PDF parsing library to each file path row in a DataFrame
Use the Azure AI Foundry SQL COPY INTO command to load PDFs directly into a knowledge-store dataset
Use MLflow model serving to parse PDFs asynchronously

questions.explanation: B is correct because wrapping a PDF parsing library (e.g., PyMuPDF or pdfplumber) in a Spark UDF allows Azure AI Foundry to distribute the parsing work across the cluster, enabling parallel ingestion of many files. A is wrong because a sequential single-node script will not scale to large document collections. C is wrong because COPY INTO is designed for structured data formats (CSV, Parquet, JSON), not binary PDF files. D is wrong because model serving is for real-time inference, not batch document processing.
questions.reference: Preprocessing pipeline: parallelizing with Spark UDFs

questions.stem: An engineer is creating a knowledge-store dataset to serve as the source for a Azure AI Search vector index index. The team needs to store document text, embeddings, and metadata. Which table schema requirement is MANDATORY for Azure AI Search vector index to work?

options.text: The table must have a column named 'embedding' of type ARRAY<FLOAT>
The table must have a primary key column of type STRING
The table must use the PARQUET file format instead of Delta
The table must have Change Data Feed disabled to prevent sync conflicts

questions.explanation: B is correct because Azure AI Search vector index requires a primary key column of type STRING to uniquely identify each document chunk. This ID column is used for upserts and deletes when syncing changes from the source knowledge-store dataset. A is wrong because the embedding column name is not required to be 'embedding' — it can be any name specified when creating the index. C is wrong because Azure AI Search vector index is designed to work with knowledge-store datasets (not raw Parquet). D is wrong because Azure AI Search vector index actually requires Change Data Feed to be enabled on the source knowledge-store dataset so that incremental changes can be tracked.
questions.reference: knowledge-store datasets for vector indexing: schema requirements

questions.stem: A team is using LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter to chunk a collection of technical manuals. They set chunk_size=512 and chunk_overlap=50. What does the chunk_overlap parameter accomplish?

options.text: It duplicates chunks that appear more than once in the document to improve recall
It ensures that each chunk shares 50 characters with the adjacent chunk, reducing the risk of splitting a sentence or concept across two chunks with no bridging context
It limits the maximum number of overlapping documents returned by the vector search
It sets the number of tokens by which embeddings from adjacent chunks are averaged together

questions.explanation: B is correct because chunk_overlap causes adjacent chunks to share a specified number of characters (or tokens) at their boundaries. This prevents important information from being lost when a concept or sentence spans a chunk boundary — the repeated text at the overlap ensures both chunks contain the bridging context. A is wrong because deduplication is a separate process. C is wrong because retrieval top-k is a separate parameter. D is wrong because embeddings are generated per chunk independently; they are not averaged with neighbors.
questions.reference: Text splitting: chunk size vs overlap tradeoffs

questions.stem: A team is preparing a dataset of customer support tickets for a RAG index. Select TWO data quality issues that are MOST important to address before embedding and indexing. (Select TWO)

options.text: Duplicate tickets that would create redundant chunks and skew retrieval results toward repeated content
Tickets written in multiple human languages, which should be normalized to English
Tickets that contain customer PII (names, account numbers) that could be exposed in LLM responses
Tickets with timestamps older than 5 years that may contain outdated information

questions.explanation: A is correct because duplicate content in the index wastes index capacity and causes retrieved results to over-represent repeated information, hurting the diversity and relevance of the context provided to the LLM. C is correct because indexing PII means it can be surfaced in LLM-generated responses to other users, creating a serious privacy and compliance risk. B is situationally relevant but not universally the most important — multilingual RAG is a valid design; normalizing to English could lose nuance. D is situationally relevant but depends on the use case — historical context can be valuable, and filtering by date is a retrieval-time decision that can use metadata filters.
questions.reference: Data quality for RAG: deduplication, PII handling

questions.stem: An engineer wants to embed documents using the BGE-Large model via Azure OpenAI and Azure AI Foundry model inference APIs and store the results in a knowledge-store dataset. Which approach correctly generates embeddings in batch using mlflow.deployments?

options.text: Use spark.ml.feature.Word2Vec to embed each document in a Spark pipeline
Call mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client('databricks').predict(endpoint='databricks-bge-large-en', inputs={'input': [text1, text2]}) to embed a batch of texts
Use mlflow.log_param('embedding_model', 'bge-large') to record the model choice, then call the model directly via REST API
Deploy BGE-Large as a custom Model Serving endpoint and call it with raw HTTP from a Spark UDF

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client('databricks') provides a client to call Azure AI Foundry-managed embedding endpoints (including Foundation Model API endpoints like databricks-bge-large-en) with a batch of texts. The predict() method handles batching and returns embedding vectors. A is wrong because Word2Vec is a traditional shallow embedding method, not a modern transformer-based embedding API call. C is wrong because mlflow.log_param only records metadata for experiment tracking; it does not call any model. D is not wrong in principle but is a roundabout approach — the mlflow.deployments client is the idiomatic Azure AI Foundry way and handles authentication and endpoint routing.
questions.reference: Embedding models in Azure AI Foundry: batch embedding with mlflow.deployments

questions.stem: A team is building a GenAI application that must answer questions about a company's internal HR policies. The policies change quarterly. Which architecture component ensures the LLM has access to the most current policy text without requiring retraining?

options.text: A fine-tuned LLM trained on the latest policy documents
A vector index populated from current policy documents, used as the retrieval component in a RAG pipeline
A larger LLM with more parameters to improve knowledge retention
An in-context learning approach where policies are pasted directly into the model weights

questions.explanation: B is correct because a vector index populated from current documents can be refreshed whenever policies are updated, allowing a RAG pipeline to always serve the latest information without modifying the LLM. A is wrong because fine-tuning is expensive and slow — quarterly retraining would be cost-prohibitive and still has a lag. C is wrong because larger models have more general knowledge but still have a training cutoff date and no awareness of internal documents. D is wrong because information cannot be 'pasted into model weights' — in-context learning means providing examples in the prompt, which is limited by context window size.
questions.reference: RAG Architecture: when to use RAG vs fine-tuning

questions.stem: An engineer is designing an embedding strategy for a RAG system that will search across both scientific papers and short news headlines. The engineer considers using a single embedding model for both. What is the PRIMARY risk of this approach?

options.text: The embedding model may have a maximum token limit that excludes full scientific papers
A single generic embedding model may not capture domain-specific terminology from scientific literature, leading to poor retrieval quality for technical queries
Using one embedding model means the system cannot support cosine similarity search
A single model increases the index size unnecessarily

questions.explanation: B is correct because a generic embedding model (e.g., trained on web text) may not adequately represent domain-specific vocabulary and concepts from scientific literature, resulting in poor semantic matching for technical queries. Domain-specific or specialized embedding models (e.g., BioBERT for biomedical text) produce better representations for specialized domains. A is a real technical constraint (max token limits exist) but it is addressed by chunking, not by using multiple models. C is wrong because the similarity metric is independent of the embedding model count. D is wrong because using one model does not affect index size — index size depends on the number of vectors and their dimensionality.
questions.reference: Embeddings: domain-specific embedding considerations

questions.stem: A Azure AI developer creates a application-managed vector index in Azure AI Search vector index. How does this index type differ from a indexer-driven vector index in terms of data management?

options.text: A application-managed vector index automatically syncs with a source knowledge-store dataset; a indexer-driven vector index requires manual API calls to update

A application-managed vector index requires the application to explicitly upsert and delete vectors via API; a indexer-driven vector index automatically tracks changes in the source knowledge-store dataset
A application-managed vector index only supports exact search; a indexer-driven vector index supports approximate nearest neighbor search
A application-managed vector index stores raw text; a indexer-driven vector index stores pre-computed embeddings

questions.explanation: B is correct and accurately describes the two index types. A application-managed vector index is managed by the application — you call the API to upsert or delete individual vectors, giving you fine-grained control. A indexer-driven vector index is managed by a pipeline that automatically watches for changes in the source knowledge-store dataset (via Change Data Feed) and propagates them to the index. A is the reverse of the correct description. C is wrong because the search algorithm (ANN vs exact) is configurable independently of index type. D is wrong because both index types can store embeddings; the distinction is about update management.
questions.reference: Vector search design: Azure AI Search vector index index types

questions.stem: A team is deciding on a chunk size for their RAG system that indexes internal engineering runbooks. Select TWO consequences of using very small chunks (e.g., 50 tokens) compared to larger chunks (e.g., 512 tokens). (Select TWO)

options.text: Very small chunks lack sufficient context to be independently meaningful, causing the LLM to receive fragments that require surrounding text to be understood
Very small chunks result in higher retrieval precision because each chunk covers a single fact
Very small chunks increase the total number of vectors in the index, increasing storage and search cost
Very small chunks eliminate the need for chunk overlap

questions.explanation: A is correct because a 50-token chunk is often too short to convey a complete thought — it may contain half a sentence or an incomplete instruction, making the retrieved context confusing to the LLM even if it is topically relevant. C is correct because smaller chunks produce more chunks per document, multiplying the number of vectors that must be stored and searched. B is wrong because very small chunks do not reliably increase precision — they may match a query on a superficial keyword but fail to provide useful information. D is wrong because overlap becomes even more important with very small chunks, since boundary-crossing information is more likely to be split.
questions.reference: Chunking strategies: optimal chunk sizes, impact on retrieval quality

questions.stem: A data scientist is comparing Llama 3 8B and Llama 3 70B for a RAG chatbot. The 8B model is significantly cheaper but the team is concerned about response quality. For which type of query would the 8B model be MOST likely to underperform relative to the 70B model?

options.text: Simple factual lookups where the answer is directly present in the retrieved context
Queries requiring multi-step reasoning, synthesis across multiple retrieved passages, or complex instruction following
Queries that require generating short, single-sentence responses
Queries submitted in English about well-known general topics

questions.explanation: B is correct because larger models have a significantly greater capacity for complex reasoning, instruction following, and synthesis tasks. For simple fact retrieval where the answer appears verbatim in the context, an 8B model often performs comparably to a 70B model. Multi-step reasoning, synthesis across documents, and nuanced instruction following are where scale differences are most pronounced. A, C, and D describe straightforward tasks where the smaller model is likely to perform acceptably.
questions.reference: LLM selection: model size tradeoffs (latency/cost/quality)

questions.stem: In a RAG pipeline, what is the role of the embedding model at query time?

options.text: It generates the final answer to the user's question
It converts the user's query into a vector so it can be compared against the indexed document vectors via similarity search
It reranks the retrieved documents by relevance before they are passed to the LLM
It tokenizes the retrieved documents into sub-word units for the LLM

questions.explanation: B is correct because at query time, the same embedding model used during document ingestion is applied to the user's query to produce a query vector. This vector is then compared against the stored document chunk vectors using a similarity metric (e.g., cosine similarity) to find the most semantically relevant chunks. A is wrong because generating the final answer is the role of the generator LLM. C is wrong because reranking is a separate, optional step that happens after initial retrieval (using a cross-encoder, not a bi-encoder embedding model). D is wrong because tokenization for the LLM is handled by the LLM's own tokenizer, not the embedding model.
questions.reference: RAG components: retrieval, embedding

questions.stem: An engineer is configuring Azure AI Search vector index and must choose between cosine similarity and dot product for their index. The embedding model they are using does NOT normalize output vectors to unit length. Which similarity metric is safer to use and why?

options.text: Dot product, because it is always faster to compute than cosine similarity
Cosine similarity, because it normalizes for vector magnitude, making the similarity score reflect only the angular (directional) similarity regardless of vector scale
L2 distance, because it accounts for both direction and magnitude and is always the most accurate
Dot product, because it gives higher scores to longer documents which typically contain more information

questions.explanation: B is correct because when embedding vectors are not unit-normalized, dot product scores are influenced by both direction and magnitude. Two vectors pointing in the same direction (same semantic meaning) will have different dot product scores if one has a larger magnitude — introducing a length/frequency bias. Cosine similarity divides by the product of the magnitudes, normalizing the score to reflect only the angular difference. When embeddings are pre-normalized to unit length, dot product and cosine similarity give identical results. A is wrong because the speed difference is negligible and is not a reason to compromise accuracy. C is wrong because L2 distance captures magnitude differences too and is less appropriate for semantic text matching.
questions.reference: Vector search design: similarity metrics (cosine, dot product)

questions.stem: A team is building a GenAI application where users upload personal financial documents and ask questions. They need to ensure that one user's documents are never retrievable in another user's session. Which design approach correctly implements this isolation in Azure AI Search vector index?

options.text: Create a single shared index and rely on the LLM's system prompt to instruct it not to share other users' data
Store a user_id metadata column in the index and apply a user_id filter on every similarity_search() call, enforced server-side by the application layer
Assign each user a different embedding model so their vectors are in incomparable spaces

Use Azure AI Foundry Azure AI Foundry project governance and Microsoft Entra-based access control row filters on the underlying knowledge-store dataset to auto-apply at query time without application-layer enforcement

questions.explanation: B is correct because the standard pattern for multi-tenant RAG with Azure AI Search vector index is to store user_id (or tenant_id) as a metadata column and pass it as a mandatory filter parameter on every search call. The application layer must enforce that a user's session can only supply their own user_id as a filter value. A is wrong because relying on the LLM's instruction following for security is unsafe — the LLM can be prompted to ignore such instructions. C is wrong because assigning different embedding models is operationally impractical and still does not enforce isolation at the retrieval layer. D is appealing but Azure AI Foundry project governance and Microsoft Entra-based access control row filters on the source knowledge-store dataset do not automatically propagate to the Vector Search index query layer.
questions.reference: Vector search design, Azure AI Search vector index API: filters with metadata

questions.stem: A product team wants to implement a GenAI feature where the chatbot proactively summarizes the conversation every 5 turns to stay within the context window while preserving key information. What is the main trade-off of this approach?

options.text: Summarization adds latency from the additional LLM call, and the summary may lose specific details that are needed to answer follow-up questions
Summarization permanently fine-tunes the model on the conversation, causing it to forget its original training
Summarization prevents the use of retrieval-augmented generation in subsequent turns
Summarization increases the context window requirement, making the approach counterproductive

questions.explanation: A is correct because rolling summarization introduces two costs: (1) latency from the additional LLM call needed to generate the summary, and (2) information loss — a summary compresses detail, and specific facts or nuances from earlier in the conversation may be omitted from the summary yet become relevant in later turns. B is wrong because LLM inference does not modify model weights. C is wrong because RAG retrieval is independent of how conversation history is managed. D is wrong because summarization reduces, not increases, the context being maintained — that is precisely the point.
questions.reference: Multi-turn conversations: summarization of history

questions.stem: An engineer needs to split a large collection of Markdown documentation files into chunks using LangChain. The documentation uses headers (# H1, ## H2) to organize sections. Which text splitter is MOST appropriate for preserving section boundaries?

options.text: CharacterTextSplitter with separator='\n'
MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter that splits based on Markdown header hierarchy
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with chunk_size=100
TokenTextSplitter that splits on the 512th token boundary

questions.explanation: B is correct because MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter understands the Markdown heading hierarchy and creates chunks that align with document sections, preserving the logical structure of the document. This means each chunk is a coherent section rather than an arbitrary character-count slice. A splits on newlines without understanding section boundaries, creating inconsistently sized and semantically arbitrary chunks. C splits on character count recursively without respecting Markdown structure. D splits at fixed token boundaries with no awareness of content structure.
questions.reference: Document loaders: loading Markdown; text splitting strategies

questions.stem: An engineer is building an embedding pipeline for a large corpus using the Azure AI Foundry Foundation Model API (e5-large-v2 endpoint). To maximize throughput, they want to embed multiple texts in a single API call. Which parameter controls the number of texts per request?

options.text: Pass a list of strings to the 'input' field of the request payload
Set the batch_size parameter on the endpoint configuration
Use the num_threads parameter in the mlflow.deployments.predict() call
Configure the max_tokens parameter to accommodate multiple documents

questions.explanation: A is correct because the Azure AI Foundry Foundation Model API embedding endpoint (following the OpenAI embeddings API convention) accepts a list of strings in the 'input' field of the JSON request body — each string in the list is embedded in a single API call, and the response returns a list of corresponding embedding vectors. B is wrong because batch_size is not a per-request parameter you configure on the endpoint directly. C is wrong because num_threads controls concurrency of client-side HTTP calls, not the number of texts per request. D is wrong because max_tokens controls the maximum length of each text, not how many texts are sent per request.
questions.reference: Embedding models in Azure AI Foundry: batch embedding with mlflow.deployments

questions.stem: A team discovers that their document corpus contains many near-duplicate product descriptions (slightly reworded versions of the same content). What is the BEST approach to handle this before indexing?

options.text: Index all near-duplicates to maximize recall; the vector search will score them similarly and the top result will still be correct
Apply MinHash LSH or embedding-based deduplication to identify and remove near-duplicate chunks before indexing
Increase the similarity threshold in vector search to filter out redundant results at query time
Use a larger chunk size to merge near-duplicate content into single chunks automatically

questions.explanation: B is correct because near-duplicates inflate the index unnecessarily, and at query time they consume multiple slots in the top-k results with essentially identical content, crowding out diverse relevant chunks. MinHash LSH is efficient for text deduplication at scale; embedding-based cosine similarity can catch semantic near-duplicates. A is wrong because while the top result may be correct, redundant chunks waste k-slots and reduce the diversity of context provided to the LLM. C is wrong because increasing the similarity threshold changes what is returned but does not eliminate redundancy in the index itself. D is wrong because larger chunk sizes do not merge semantically similar but textually non-adjacent documents.
questions.reference: Data quality for RAG: deduplication

questions.stem: A team is building a RAG pipeline for a customer support system. Customer emails are the source documents. Select TWO preprocessing steps that are MOST important before embedding. (Select TWO)

options.text: Remove email headers (From:, To:, Subject:, Date:) and quoted reply chains to avoid embedding boilerplate and repeated content
Convert all text to uppercase for consistent tokenization
Replace customer names, email addresses, and account numbers with placeholder tokens to prevent PII from being indexed
Translate all non-English emails to English before embedding

questions.explanation: A is correct because email headers and quoted reply chains are noise — they do not contain useful semantic content and will degrade embedding quality and waste index space. Removing them ensures the embedding represents only the substantive content. C is correct because indexing PII exposes it to being surfaced in future LLM responses to other users, creating a serious privacy and compliance risk (GDPR, CCPA). B is wrong because converting to uppercase can actually hurt tokenization for modern subword tokenizers and provides no quality benefit. D is situationally useful but not universally necessary — multilingual embedding models can handle multiple languages, and translation introduces potential errors and loses nuance.
questions.reference: Data quality for RAG: noise removal, PII handling, metadata extraction

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to enable Azure AI Search vector index Delta Sync on a knowledge-store dataset. When they try to create the index, they receive an error saying the source table is not supported. What is the MOST likely reason?

options.text: The table is in a different database schema than the Vector Search endpoint
The knowledge-store dataset does not have Change Data Feed (CDF) enabled, which is required for Delta Sync to track incremental changes
The table contains more than 1 million rows, exceeding the Delta Sync limit
The table uses ZORDER optimization, which is incompatible with Vector Search indexing

questions.explanation: B is correct because Azure AI Search vector index Delta Sync requires the source knowledge-store dataset to have Change Data Feed (CDF) enabled (ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableChangeDataFeed' = 'true')). Without CDF, the sync pipeline cannot track which rows have been inserted, updated, or deleted since the last sync. A is wrong because cross-schema access is supported given appropriate permissions. C is wrong because there is no documented row count limit that small for indexer-driven vector indexes. D is wrong because ZORDER optimization is compatible with Vector Search; it only affects the physical layout of the knowledge-store dataset files for Spark queries.
questions.reference: knowledge-store datasets for vector indexing: schema requirements

questions.stem: An agent must always answer in JSON with fields `answer` and `citations`. Where should that requirement be placed for the MOST consistent behavior?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instructions
Only in the final user message
Inside retrieved documents
Inside a warehouse policy comment

questions.explanation: Persistent formatting and behavioral constraints belong in the system-level instructions because they govern every turn. Retrieved documents and user messages are less reliable for durable control.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry Context Engineer certification page; prompt design guidance

questions.stem: A prompt engineer keeps adding long persona text that does not affect task quality but consumes tokens. What is the BEST improvement?

options.text: Keep only instructions that materially change agent behavior and remove decorative prompt text
Add more adjectives so the agent stays in character
Move the persona text into every retrieved chunk
Replace task instructions with chain-of-thought requirements

questions.explanation: Context engineering emphasizes useful signal in the context window. Extra decorative persona text consumes tokens without helping task completion.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry certification page; context window management guidance

questions.stem: A team wants a managed semantic search index that stays synchronized with a knowledge-store dataset automatically. Which Azure AI Foundry option BEST fits?

options.text: A delta-sync Vector Search index
A notebook-scoped pandas DataFrame
A SQL warehouse result cache
A model registry alias

questions.explanation: Delta-sync indexes keep a vector search index synchronized with a source knowledge-store dataset. The other options are not managed semantic retrieval indexes.
questions.reference: Azure AI Search vector index documentation

questions.stem: Search quality drops because each chunk contains many unrelated topics from long documents. Which change is MOST likely to help?

options.text: Use a chunking strategy that preserves local semantic coherence
Increase warehouse auto-stop timeout
Disable embeddings and use only row-level security
Store every document as one very large chunk

questions.explanation: Better chunking improves retrieval because embeddings then represent one coherent topic instead of several unrelated ones. Very large chunks usually hurt recall and precision.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry RAG and vector search guidance

questions.stem: A retrieval system needs to balance semantic similarity with exact keyword matching for product codes. Select TWO approaches that BEST help. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use hybrid retrieval that combines dense and sparse signals
Use re-ranking to improve the final ordering of retrieved results
Remove all metadata fields from the index
Increase the model temperature during generation

questions.explanation: Hybrid retrieval helps with exact tokens like product IDs while re-ranking improves final relevance ordering. Metadata removal and generation temperature do not solve retrieval ranking.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry AI Search / Vector Search guidance; retrieval best practices

questions.stem: A support agent must remember important user preferences across separate sessions days apart. Which memory design is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Persist long-term memory outside the immediate chat window and retrieve it when relevant
Keep all historical turns in the active prompt forever
Rely only on the model's pretraining
Use a larger warehouse for every query

questions.explanation: Cross-session memory requires external persistence and selective retrieval. Keeping everything in-context forever is expensive and eventually impossible because of token limits.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry agent systems guidance; memory architecture concepts

questions.stem: Conversation history is causing prompt bloat, but the agent still needs prior decisions. Which technique BEST addresses this?

options.text: Summarize older turns into a compact state representation
Duplicate the entire chat transcript in the system prompt
Delete all history after every message
Move the history into column comments

questions.explanation: Summarization preserves key facts while reducing token usage. Duplicating the transcript worsens prompt bloat, and deleting everything loses useful context.
questions.reference: Context window management and memory summarization guidance

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY value of exposing a well-defined tool schema to an agent?

options.text: It lets the model understand the tool's inputs and outputs so it can call the tool more reliably
It guarantees the model will never hallucinate
It replaces authentication and governance controls
It eliminates the need for prompt instructions

questions.explanation: A clear tool schema improves reliable tool invocation by making inputs and outputs explicit. It does not replace governance or eliminate all model errors.
questions.reference: Model Context Protocol concepts; Azure AI Foundry agent tooling guidance

questions.stem: A governed analytics agent should query sensitive tables only through approved interfaces. Which design BEST supports this goal?

options.text: Expose curated Azure AI Foundry project governance and Microsoft Entra-based access control functions or governed tools instead of giving the model unrestricted raw table access
Let the model generate arbitrary SQL against every catalog
Embed secrets in the prompt
Bypass Azure AI Foundry project governance and Microsoft Entra-based access control and read cloud storage directly

questions.explanation: Governed tools and functions constrain what the agent can do and allow policy enforcement. Arbitrary access and embedded secrets create unnecessary risk.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry project governance and Microsoft Entra-based access control functions; Azure AI Foundry governance for AI applications

questions.stem: A team is integrating external tools into an agent. Select TWO good practices. (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool inputs against the declared schema
Return clear error information so the agent can recover or ask for clarification
Hide tool side effects from logs to save storage
Make every tool accept one unstructured free-text string only

questions.explanation: Input validation and clear error handling improve reliability and recovery. Opaque free-text tool signatures and missing observability make tool use harder to govern.
questions.reference: Tool design and MCP-style integration best practices

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes token budgeting in a production agent?

options.text: You must account for instructions, retrieved context, tool traces, and conversation history within the model limit
Only user messages count toward context length
Tokens matter only for cost, not model limits
Result caching eliminates token limits

questions.explanation: All prompt components consume context-window capacity, so token budgeting is a core context-engineering task. Caches do not remove model limits.
questions.reference: Azure AI Foundry context engineering page; LLM context window basics

questions.stem: An agent frequently overflows its context window after retrieving too many chunks. Which mitigation is MOST targeted?

options.text: Reduce and prioritize retrieved context, then summarize or prune lower-value history
Increase response temperature
Store chunks in a second notebook
Disable citations

questions.explanation: Overflow is best addressed by better retrieval selection and prompt compaction. Temperature and citations do not meaningfully reduce context size.
questions.reference: RAG and context compaction guidance
